summary: Gruber reviews Apple's new standalone Sports app, praising its speed, simplicity, and focused design. He notes it loads nearly instantaneously in contrast to bloated competitors like ESPN, and includes betting odds from DraftKings by default. The app is iPhone-only at launch (like Journal), which Gruber finds puzzling, and it effectively sherlocks third-party apps like Sports Alerts. He argues the decision to make Sports a standalone app rather than folding it into TV or News exemplifies the iPhone philosophy of smaller, more focused apps over monolithic ones.
key_points: Apple Sports differentiates itself primarily through speed — loading nearly instantaneously while competitors like ESPN are bogged down by ad tech and poor programming
The app includes DraftKings betting odds by default, though Gruber notes their odds tend to be outliers compared to FanDuel or BetMGM consensus lines
The app effectively sherlocks third-party sports score apps like Sports Alerts, which as a cross-platform app can't match a truly iOS-native design
Making Sports a standalone app rather than part of TV or News embodies the iPhone philosophy of smaller, focused apps over bigger monolithic ones
The design language is new and extremely simple — three tabs (Yesterday, Today, Upcoming) with swipeable event cards — achieving the kind of simplicity that takes the most work
money_quotes: Just being very fast to load ought not be a hugely differentiating factor in 2024, but it is.
Ad tech, combined with poor programming, has made most sports apps slow to load. Most apps, period, really.
It's so simple and intuitive that it doesn't seem designed at all, but that's the sort of design that takes the most work and most iteration.
A truly iOS-native live sports scores/stats app ought to be able to blow anything cross-platform out of the water, and Apple Sports seems to be that.
The iPhone way of thinking is to have more apps that are smaller and more focused, not fewer apps that are bigger and more monolithic.
There's a natural tendency in our industry — especially from big companies — to keep adding more and more features to existing apps and services.
Apple Sports exemplifies why it's a better idea to design smaller, more focused apps.
It's very rare for a new iPhone app, immediately upon debut, to land a spot on my first iPhone Home Screen. But Journal did, and so now has Sports.
key_insight: Apple Sports succeeds by rejecting the industry tendency toward bloated, monolithic apps, instead delivering a focused, blindingly fast experience that demonstrates how speed and simplicity remain the most powerful differentiators in app design.
